title: 'Complacent' super giants lose $8b in a year
full_text:
Australians transferred nearly $8 billion out of the country's largest pension funds and into self-managed alternatives over the past financial year, accelerating a trend that analysts say shows broader disengagement and complacency across the retirement income industry.
Analysis of Australian Prudential Regulation Authority data by Elula - an AI software developer for the financial services sector - shows net transfers to SMSFs increased 40 per cent in the year to June 30, 2025, with $2.8 billion leaving dominant players AustralianSuper and Australian Retirement Trust.
They are the two single biggest superannuation funds in the country, managing a combined $750 billion.
Although they represent a small proportion of their total assets, the accelerating number of members choosing to go it alone has become a headache for the funds as they enter "competitive outflow", where more members leave than join.
It is a position ART has been in for several years, but AustralianSuper only entered this territory for the first time in more than 20 years last financial year. Elula chief executive Josh Shipman said members were disenchanted with big funds and their services and were turning to financial advisers.
"Members are talking to financial advisers, absent of any direction from their fund, and the result is that they are then leaving and setting up an SMSF," he said, adding the APRA data also indicated there were 3.7 million inactive accounts across the sector that had not taken a contribution in over a year.
"Super funds have been complacent on both levels: the member level and the adviser level," he said. "Mark my words, the churn will be much higher on every metric [in financial year 2026]."
Industry super funds, in particular, have found themselves under siege from competitors as more older, wealthier Australians seek out financial advice, though the focus has largely been on the flight to platforms.
Wealth management platforms are run by retail for-profit funds and offer a virtual storefront of trustee-approved investment options to choose from when allocating a client's savings.
Industry super funds lost about $6.2 billion to SMSFs last financial year, and retail funds lost about $1.7 billion. Despite big corporate master trusts such as MLC and Colonial First State losing members to this market, this was offset by significant flows in the opposite direction - from SMSFs to the fund - via their platform businesses, which come under different names in the APRA data.
MLC-owner Insignia also controls IOOF Portfolio Services, which recorded net inflows of $110 million from SMSFs, according to the Elula analysis. HUB24, which operates one of the biggest wealth platforms in Australia, received $644 millions from SMSFs.
Andrew Inwood, who runs market research firm CoreData, said the flight to SMSFs was being accelerated by younger professionals about a decade out of university. "They come from specialist employment spaces - think doctors or engineers - [and] are growing in that space because they see greater control, choice and there is also a bit of a hedonistic vice; they enjoy running them," he said.
Inwood said super funds had to offer more investment choices within the fund to keep younger members. For older members, they had to boost engagement and offer in-house advice services, he said.
"They are starting to understand all of this," Inwood said. "But the numbers are so significant, it is hard to arrest. This is ultimately a momentum thing for the entire industry."
With 14,500 SMSFs created in the September quarter alone, SMSF Association boss Peter Burgess said it was likely 2026 would mark the third consecutive year of record growth for the self-managed sector.
He said the main factor driving people to SMSFs was greater choice.
"There is a structural change on the way with Australians turning to SMSFs for more tailored and personalised outcomes."
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Lucas Baird
heading: Superannuation and Investments
